chapters.what-is-a-fork: A live database and its twin

events.text: Meet the app: demo-pgadmin, a PostgreSQL with a pgAdmin interface, mounted and running.
events.afterText: One live repo. Let's clone it.
events.prose: Start from a live repository: a PostgreSQL database with pgAdmin running on the machine.

events.text: Fork it. The copy-on-write clone takes seconds regardless of size; detach returns as soon as the containers start, while health checks continue in the background.
events.afterText: Forked and starting. The data did not get copied — it got shared, until one side changes it.
events.prose: Fork the repository with --up --detach: the CoW clone is near-instant and the fork's services start immediately.

events.text: Two repositories now: the original and the experiment, each with its own containers, network, and disk view.
events.prose: List the repositories: the original and the fork run side by side, fully isolated.

events.text: Done experimenting? Delete the fork. Seconds to create, seconds to throw away — the original never knew it existed.
events.afterText: Gone. The original kept serving the whole time.
events.prose: Delete the fork when you are done. The original repository is untouched.

narrations.text: Welcome back! Last time you saw the fork commands. Now let's watch what they actually mean: a live database app, copied in seconds, edited freely, while the original never notices a thing.
Here's the setup: a real PostgreSQL with a pgAdmin interface on top, live on the server. A fork is an instant copy-on-write twin of all of it. Same data at birth, completely separate lives after.

This is the original, in the browser. pgAdmin asks for a login, because it's the production copy, and production stays guarded. Once we're in, let's open the query tool and look at the data: a tasks table with four real rows.
And here's the fork's pgAdmin. No login this time: forks are disposable sandboxes, so the gate steps aside. Run the same query, and there they are, the same four tasks. The fork inherited every row.

Now side by side: the fork on the left, the original on the right. Watch this. On the fork, we drop the entire tasks table. Gone, the query now errors. Then we re-run the query on the original: all four rows, untouched. That's isolation you can see.
So fork freely, production can't get hurt. Next up, we look at secrets, the one thing your forks deliberately don't inherit. See you there!
